@@ -241,7 +241,7 @@ {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-ori","title":"Task: Create lesson progress tracking system","description":"# Create Lesson Progress Tracking System ## Parent Feature Learning Hub Core Infrastructure (tor) ## Goal Implement a TanStack Query + localStorage based progress tracking system for the 9 lessons, following the pattern established by `lib/wizardSteps.ts`. ## Implementation ### File: `apps/web/lib/lessonProgress.ts` ```typescript import { useQuery, useMutation, useQueryClient } from "@tanstack/react-query"; import { useCallback } from "react"; import { safeGetJSON, safeSetJSON } from "./utils"; export interface Lesson { id: number; // 0-8 slug: string; // URL-friendly name title: string; // Display title file: string; // Source markdown file duration: string; // Estimated reading time description: string; // Brief summary } export const LESSONS: Lesson[] = [ { id: 0, slug: 'welcome', title: 'Welcome & Overview', file: '00_welcome.md', duration: '5 min', description: 'Understand what you have and what you will learn' }, { id: 1, slug: 'linux-basics', title: 'Linux Navigation', file: '01_linux_basics.md', duration: '8 min', description: 'Navigate the filesystem with confidence' }, { id: 2, slug: 'ssh-basics', title: 'SSH & Persistence', file: '02_ssh_basics.md', duration: '6 min', description: 'Stay connected and understand SSH' }, { id: 3, slug: 'tmux-basics', title: 'tmux Basics', file: '03_tmux_basics.md', duration: '7 min', description: 'Master persistent terminal sessions' }, { id: 4, slug: 'agent-commands', title: 'Agent Commands', file: '04_agents_login.md', duration: '10 min', description: 'Login and use your coding agents' }, { id: 5, slug: 'ntm-core', title: 'NTM Command Center', file: '05_ntm_core.md', duration: '8 min', description: 'Orchestrate agent sessions' }, { id: 6, slug: 'ntm-palette', title: 'NTM Prompt Palette', file: '06_ntm_command_palette.md', duration: '6 min', description: 'Use pre-built prompts effectively' }, { id: 7, slug: 'flywheel-loop', title: 'The Flywheel Loop', file: '07_flywheel_loop.md', duration: '10 min', description: 'Understand the complete workflow' }, { id: 8, slug: 'keeping-updated', title: 'Keeping Updated', file: '08_keeping_updated.md', duration: '4 min', description: 'Keep your tools current' }, ]; export const TOTAL_LESSONS = LESSONS.length; export const COMPLETED_LESSONS_KEY = "agent-flywheel-lessons-completed"; export const lessonKeys = { completedLessons: ["lessons", "completed"] as const, }; export function getLessonById(id: number): Lesson | undefined { return LESSONS.find(l => l.id === id); } export function getLessonBySlug(slug: string): Lesson | undefined { return LESSONS.find(l => l.slug === slug); } export function getCompletedLessons(): number[] { const parsed = safeGetJSON(COMPLETED_LESSONS_KEY); if (Array.isArray(parsed)) { return parsed.filter((n): n is number => typeof n === "number" && n >= 0 && n < TOTAL_LESSONS ); } return []; } export function setCompletedLessons(lessons: number[]): void { const valid = lessons.filter(n => n >= 0 && n < TOTAL_LESSONS); safeSetJSON(COMPLETED_LESSONS_KEY, valid); } export function useCompletedLessons(): [number[], (lessonId: number) => void] { const queryClient = useQueryClient(); const { data: lessons } = useQuery({ queryKey: lessonKeys.completedLessons, queryFn: getCompletedLessons, staleTime: Infinity, gcTime: Infinity, }); const mutation = useMutation({ mutationFn: async (lessonId: number) => { const current = getCompletedLessons(); if (current.includes(lessonId)) return current; const updated = [...current, lessonId].sort((a, b) => a - b); setCompletedLessons(updated); return updated; }, onSuccess: (updated) => { queryClient.setQueryData(lessonKeys.completedLessons, updated); }, }); const markComplete = useCallback((lessonId: number) => { mutation.mutate(lessonId); }, [mutation]); return [lessons ?? [], markComplete]; } // Helper for checking if a lesson is complete export function isLessonComplete(lessonId: number): boolean { return getCompletedLessons().includes(lessonId); } // Get next uncompleted lesson export function getNextLesson(): Lesson | undefined { const completed = getCompletedLessons(); return LESSONS.find(l => !completed.includes(l.id)); } ``` ## Testing Create `apps/web/lib/__tests__/lessonProgress.test.ts` with unit tests for: - `getLessonById` / `getLessonBySlug` - `getCompletedLessons` / `setCompletedLessons` - `getNextLesson` ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] All 9 lessons defined with correct metadata - [ ] Hook works identically to wizard progress hook - [ ] Progress persists across page reloads - [ ] TypeScript types are properly exported ## File Changes - Create: `apps/web/lib/lessonProgress.ts` - Create: `apps/web/lib/__tests__/lessonProgress.test.ts` (optional)","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T18:19:02.691384-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T18:25:36.032502-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T18:25:36.032502-05:00","close_reason":"Implementation complete - lessonProgress.ts already exists with all 9 lessons, TanStack Query hooks, localStorage persistence, and TypeScript types. Verified type-check passes.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-ori","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-tor","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T18:24:06.880428-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-pom","title":"Implement ACFS self-update","description":"## What Before updating other components, acfs-update should update itself: 1. git pull in ~/.acfs (or wherever ACFS is installed) 2. Re-source the update script if it changed 3. Continue with other updates using latest logic ## Why This Matters - Ensures we have the latest update logic before updating tools - Fixes any bugs in the update process itself - Keeps checksums.yaml current (though not used for updates) ## Technical Details - ACFS installed at ~/.acfs or detected via ACFS_HOME env var - git pull --ff-only to avoid merge conflicts - If update script changed, need to re-exec with same args ## Considerations - What if git pull fails? (network, conflicts) - Should we require this, or make it optional? - Re-exec is tricky - need to preserve all args and state ## Decision Make self-update automatic but non-fatal. If it fails, warn and continue with current version. ## Success Criteria - [ ] ACFS self-updates on each run - [ ] Handles git pull failures gracefully - [ ] Re-exec works if script changed (or skip re-exec v1)","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T13:26:45.271437-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T13:31:43.349328-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T13:31:43.349328-05:00","close_reason":"Out of scope for v1. ACFS is not git-cloned to ~/.acfs, so self-update requires re-running the installer. Users can do this manually. Consider for v2 after changing install to use git clone.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-pom","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-75e","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T13:27:25.670496-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-q10","title":"Harden installer: true dry-run, onboard source, HTTPS PGDG","description":"Fix install.sh --dry-run so it makes no system changes and works without sudo; install onboard command from packages/onboard/onboard.sh (single source of truth); switch PGDG apt repo URL to HTTPS; fix checksum computation in scripts/lib/security.sh.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"FuchsiaCreek","created_at":"2025-12-20T13:18:40.054877-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-20T13:22:16.261425-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-20T13:22:16.261425-05:00","close_reason":"Fixed install.sh dry-run to make no system changes + allow running without sudo; install onboard from packages/onboard; switch PGDG repo to HTTPS; fix security.sh checksum logic."} -{"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-q7r3","title":"[EPIC] First SSH Connection Experience","description":"# Epic: First SSH Connection Experience ## Problem Statement The first SSH connection is where the most beginners fail and abandon the process. Three critical issues: 1. **"Type yes" terror**: The host authenticity warning looks like a SECURITY ALERT. Users are afraid to type "yes" because it looks like they're accepting malware. 2. **Password confusion**: Users confuse their VPS provider account password (Hetzner login) with the VPS root password (set during VPS creation). These are DIFFERENT passwords. 3. **Post-connection disorientation**: User successfully connects, sees "root@vps:~#", has no idea what this means or that they're now "inside" the VPS. ## Background & Reasoning SSH is a completely foreign concept. "Secure Shell" means nothing to beginners. The mental model of "remote controlling another computer through text" is not intuitive. The host authenticity prompt is particularly problematic because it uses security language ("ECDSA key fingerprint", "Are you sure you want to continue?") that sounds dangerous. Security-conscious users will hesitate; naive users will learn bad habits. The password confusion is a classic support issue. Users try their Hetzner account password 3 times, get locked out, and panic. ## User Story As a beginner making my first SSH connection, I want clear guidance through each prompt and potential error, So that I can successfully connect without fear or confusion. ## Success Criteria 1. User understands the "type yes" prompt is normal and safe 2. User knows which password to use (VPS root, not provider account) 3. User understands they're now "inside" the VPS after connecting 4. User can verify they're on the VPS (e.g., run hostname) 5. User knows how to disconnect (exit) ## Scope - Enhance ssh-connect wizard page with more detailed guidance - Add prominent password clarification - Add post-connection verification step - Improve troubleshooting for common errors - Add mental model explanation for "being inside" the VPS ## Dependencies - Epic A: Terminal Onboarding Foundation (user must understand terminal) - Epic B: SSH Key Generation (user must have generated key) ## UI/UX Requirements - Maintain current polish for desktop and mobile - Password clarification should be PROMINENT, not buried in SimplerGuide - Use AlertCard for critical warnings (which password to use) - Consider showing the scary prompt so users recognize it ## Technical Approach 1. Add AlertCard above the SSH command explaining password distinction 2. Add OutputPreview showing the "type yes" prompt with explanation 3. Add post-connection verification: "Try running: hostname" 4. Enhance troubleshooting with password-specific guidance 5. Add "You're now inside the VPS" confirmation section ## Estimated Effort Medium (1 wizard page major enhancement) ## Priority Justification P0 (Critical) - This is the highest drop-off point in the wizard. Users who fail here never complete setup.","status":"in_progress","priority":0,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:34:56.174103-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T14:45:05.847579-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-q7r3","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-ux1f","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:37:43.878369-05:00","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-q7r3","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-1wk6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:37:49.119933-05:00","created_by":"jemanuel"}]} +{"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-q7r3","title":"[EPIC] First SSH Connection Experience","description":"# Epic: First SSH Connection Experience ## Problem Statement The first SSH connection is where the most beginners fail and abandon the process. Three critical issues: 1. **"Type yes" terror**: The host authenticity warning looks like a SECURITY ALERT. Users are afraid to type "yes" because it looks like they're accepting malware. 2. **Password confusion**: Users confuse their VPS provider account password (Hetzner login) with the VPS root password (set during VPS creation). These are DIFFERENT passwords. 3. **Post-connection disorientation**: User successfully connects, sees "root@vps:~#", has no idea what this means or that they're now "inside" the VPS. ## Background & Reasoning SSH is a completely foreign concept. "Secure Shell" means nothing to beginners. The mental model of "remote controlling another computer through text" is not intuitive. The host authenticity prompt is particularly problematic because it uses security language ("ECDSA key fingerprint", "Are you sure you want to continue?") that sounds dangerous. Security-conscious users will hesitate; naive users will learn bad habits. The password confusion is a classic support issue. Users try their Hetzner account password 3 times, get locked out, and panic. ## User Story As a beginner making my first SSH connection, I want clear guidance through each prompt and potential error, So that I can successfully connect without fear or confusion. ## Success Criteria 1. User understands the "type yes" prompt is normal and safe 2. User knows which password to use (VPS root, not provider account) 3. User understands they're now "inside" the VPS after connecting 4. User can verify they're on the VPS (e.g., run hostname) 5. User knows how to disconnect (exit) ## Scope - Enhance ssh-connect wizard page with more detailed guidance - Add prominent password clarification - Add post-connection verification step - Improve troubleshooting for common errors - Add mental model explanation for "being inside" the VPS ## Dependencies - Epic A: Terminal Onboarding Foundation (user must understand terminal) - Epic B: SSH Key Generation (user must have generated key) ## UI/UX Requirements - Maintain current polish for desktop and mobile - Password clarification should be PROMINENT, not buried in SimplerGuide - Use AlertCard for critical warnings (which password to use) - Consider showing the scary prompt so users recognize it ## Technical Approach 1. Add AlertCard above the SSH command explaining password distinction 2. Add OutputPreview showing the "type yes" prompt with explanation 3. Add post-connection verification: "Try running: hostname" 4. Enhance troubleshooting with password-specific guidance 5. Add "You're now inside the VPS" confirmation section ## Estimated Effort Medium (1 wizard page major enhancement) ## Priority Justification P0 (Critical) - This is the highest drop-off point in the wizard. Users who fail here never complete setup.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:34:56.174103-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T14:48:02.756085-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-22T14:48:02.756085-05:00","close_reason":"Implemented: Added prominent password distinction warning (VPS root vs provider account), 'type yes' prompt preview with explanation, password prompt with invisible typing note. All success criteria met.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-q7r3","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-ux1f","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:37:43.878369-05:00","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-q7r3","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-1wk6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:37:49.119933-05:00","created_by":"jemanuel"}]} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-ql6q","title":"Explain RAM and specs in practical user terms","description":"# Task: Explain RAM and Specs in User Terms ## Parent Epic [EPIC] VPS Conceptual Framework ## Problem "16GB RAM" and "Ubuntu 24.04" are meaningless to beginners. They don't know: - What RAM means in practical terms - Why more is better for AI agents - What Ubuntu version numbers mean - What to do if their provider doesn't have 25.10 ## Implementation Details Location: apps/web/app/wizard/create-vps/page.tsx Enhance the checklist and SimplerGuide: \`\`\`tsx

RAM (Memory)

RAM is your VPS's "working memory" — how many things it can think about at once. More RAM = more AI agents running simultaneously.

  • 8GB: Minimum. One AI agent at a time.
  • 16GB: Comfortable. 2-3 agents working together.
  • 32GB: Ideal. Run agent swarms without worry.

Ubuntu Version

Ubuntu is a type of Linux (the operating system). The numbers are version identifiers (like iPhone 15 vs iPhone 14). Pick the highest number available. Our installer will upgrade to 25.10 if needed.

\`\`\` ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] RAM explained with practical examples (agents = tasks) - [ ] Clear tier recommendations (8/16/32GB) - [ ] Ubuntu versions demystified - [ ] Reassurance about installer handling upgrades ## Why This Matters Users who don't understand specs either over-buy (wasting money) or under-buy (causing performance issues later).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:54:26.539739-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T14:02:06.572034-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-22T14:02:06.572034-05:00","close_reason":"Already covered: rent-vps/page.tsx has extensive 'Understanding the specs' and 'Why 64GB RAM?' sections"} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-qqo","title":"Create error context tracking globals and try_step() wrapper","description":"# Task: Create error context tracking globals and try_step() wrapper ## Context Part of EPIC: Per-Phase Error Reporting (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-fkf) ## What to Do Add error context tracking to capture exactly where failures occur: ### Global Variables ```bash CURRENT_PHASE="" CURRENT_PHASE_NAME="" CURRENT_STEP="" LAST_ERROR="" LAST_ERROR_CODE="" ``` ### try_step() Function Wrapper for individual operations within a phase: ```bash try_step "description" command args... # - Sets CURRENT_STEP # - Saves state (for resume granularity) # - Captures output and exit code # - On failure: sets LAST_ERROR, returns 1 ``` ## Acceptance Criteria - Every installation command wrapped with try_step - Error output captured (not just exit code) - Current step visible in state.json for debugging - Works with pipes and complex commands ## Implementation Notes - Use eval for complex commands - Capture both stdout and stderr - Truncate very long errors for display ## Files to Modify - install.sh: Add globals and try_step function","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T12:43:57.840225-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T14:43:08.613653-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T14:43:08.613653-05:00","close_reason":"Created scripts/lib/error_tracking.sh with try_step(), try_step_optional(), try_step_retry(), phase management, and error reporting functions"} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-qup","title":"Show skipped tools in acfs doctor output","description":"# Task: Show skipped tools in acfs doctor output ## Purpose When doctor runs, clearly show which tools were intentionally skipped vs which failed unexpectedly. ## Critical Distinction: Intentional Skip vs Bug **Problem:** Without this distinction, doctor output is confusing: - User intentionally skipped `ohmyzsh` via checksum prompt - Doctor shows: `[✗] ohmyzsh: not found` - User thinks: "Did installation break?" **Solution:** Track skip reasons and display appropriately. ### Skip Tracking in state.json ```json { "skipped_tools": { "ohmyzsh": { "reason": "checksum_mismatch", "skipped_at": "2025-01-15T10:30:00Z", "user_choice": "skip" }, "atuin": { "reason": "network_error", "skipped_at": "2025-01-15T10:32:00Z", "retries": 3 } } } ``` ### Doctor Output Format ``` ACFS Doctor Report ================== Installed Tools: [✓] mise 2024.12.0 [✓] uv 0.5.0 [✓] bun 1.2.0 [✓] go 1.23.0 ... Intentionally Skipped: [○] ohmyzsh - skipped (checksum mismatch, user choice) [○] atuin - skipped (network error after 3 retries) Missing (unexpected): [✗] lazygit - not found (should be installed by mise) [✗] starship - not found Legend: [✓] installed [○] skipped [✗] missing ``` ### Key Design Decisions 1. **Use `[○]` for skipped** - visually distinct from `[✓]` (success) and `[✗]` (error) 2. **Show skip reason** - user knows WHY it was skipped 3. **Separate section** - dont mix intentional skips with failures 4. **Missing = unexpected** - tools that SHOULD exist but dont ### Implementation ```bash show_tool_status() { local tool="$1" local state_file="$ACFS_STATE_DIR/state.json" # Check if intentionally skipped if jq -e ".skipped_tools.\"$tool\"" "$state_file" &>/dev/null; then local reason=$(jq -r ".skipped_tools.\"$tool\".reason" "$state_file") echo "[○] $tool - skipped ($reason)" return fi # Check if installed if command -v "$tool" &>/dev/null; then local version=$("$tool" --version 2>/dev/null | head -1 || echo "installed") echo "[✓] $tool $version" else echo "[✗] $tool - not found" fi } ``` ## Dependencies - Depends on: state.json schema (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-5zt) - Used by: doctor output formatting (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-aqs) ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Skipped tools tracked in state.json with reason - [ ] Doctor uses [○] symbol for intentional skips - [ ] Separate "Intentionally Skipped" section in output - [ ] Skip reason displayed (checksum/network/user choice) - [ ] Clear legend explaining symbols","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T12:50:40.525291-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T15:08:37.008506-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T15:08:37.008506-05:00","close_reason":"Implemented skipped tools display with [○] indicator, legend, and JSON support","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-qup","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-8z5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T12:51:29.321854-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}
